summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's latest adjustments to its DMA compliance plan, including allowing web distribution of apps directly from developer websites, custom link-out screens, and single-developer marketplaces. The web distribution option comes with a major catch: developers must have at least one app with over one million EU installations, creating a catch-22 where you need App Store success to bypass the App Store. Gruber argues these changes almost certainly result from back-channel communications with the European Commission rather than Apple's own initiative, since Apple explicitly argued against direct downloads just six weeks earlier. He traces a pattern where policy-only changes likely came from developer feedback, while major structural changes like web distribution and the PWA reversal suggest EC pressure. The piece also debunks the narrative that Apple is trying to kill PWAs, arguing the EU-only changes were a logical consequence of DMA requirements around browser engine neutrality. Gruber concludes that whatever Apple isn't changing — like the Core Technology Fee — is either EC-approved or something Apple is willing to fight for.
key_points: Web distribution requires developers to have an app with 1M+ EU installs, creating a catch-22 where App Store success is needed to bypass the App Store

The pattern of changes suggests Apple is responding to unofficial EC feedback: policy changes came from developers, but structural changes like web distribution and PWA reversal point to regulatory pressure
Apple's PWA removal in the EU wasn't anti-web hostility but a logical consequence of DMA rules against preferencing WebKit, reversed when the EC signaled it wouldn't penalize WebKit-only PWAs
The things Apple isn't changing, like the Core Technology Fee, are either acceptable to the EC or battles Apple is willing to fight
The DMA creates an inherent tension by demanding both more openness and maintained security, and Apple is exploiting that tension to limit web distribution to established developers
money_quotes: The eligibility requirement of having an app with over 1 million annual installations in the EU is a high barrier.

It's sort of a catch-22: the entire feature is by definition intended for developers who want to distribute their apps outside Apple's App Store — but the only way to qualify is to have at least one very popular app in the App Store or an app marketplace.
If Apple had wanted to offer direct downloads of individual apps from developers' own websites, they would have included this from the start.
The DMA wants to have its cake and eat it too. It requires Apple both to open up iOS to additional methods of software distribution and to keep iOS as secure as possible.

Apple made this change not because they want to 'kill' web apps, but because they were under the impression that the DMA required them to either have no Home Screen web app support at all in the EU, or allow all third-party rendering engines to save PWAs to the Home Screen.
There exists a contingent of tinfoil-hat-wearing web app zealots who think PWAs are like this close to taking over mobile app development.
It's like desktop Linux getting popular, or Bluetooth getting reliable: always 'next year'.
The things Apple isn't changing — like the Core Technology Fee — are either OK with the EC, or, if not, that Apple is willing to fight for them.
key_insight: Apple's piecemeal DMA compliance adjustments reveal an ongoing back-channel negotiation with the EC, where Apple opened with its most restrictive plan and is ratcheting toward openness only as regulators demand it.
